title: Wet-felted soap wrap
subtitle: A merino felt sleeve felted directly around a bar of soap, 8 x 5 cm
excerpt: Felt merino roving directly around a bar of soap to produce a gentle exfoliating sleeve that lathers as the soap inside wears down. The soap acts as a resist during felting and shrinks clear of the felt as it is used. Uses around 20 g of roving per bar.
sourceNotes: Felting directly around a bar of soap is a standard introductory wet-felting project. The soap acts as a rounded resist form and also provides the soapy medium needed for felting. Observation-led; widely used in European felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for felting.

An object placed inside a wet-felting piece to create a hollow space or prevent the two sides of felt from bonding together. In soap wraps, the bar of soap is the resist; it is used up as the project is used.

content.text: In this project the bar of soap serves as both the
around which the felt forms and the source of the soapy medium that makes felting possible.

is wrapped around a dry bar of soap and felted in place using water and friction. As the bar is used in washing, the felt sleeves gently as the soap inside diminishes. The felt itself produces a light exfoliating effect. Use a solid vegetable oil-based soap, not a synthetic detergent bar: the soap oils do not damage the wool fibres and the bar stays firm through felting.

items.name: Merino roving, 21 to 24 micron, 20 g per bar
Bar of solid vegetable soap, approximately 8 x 5 x 2.5 cm
Hot water in a bowl or sink

content.text: Step 1: Wrap the soap in roving

Pull wisps of roving approximately 1.5 cm wide and wrap them lengthways around the soap bar, covering all six faces with a thin even layer. Add a second set of wisps crossways over the first. The roving should be wrapped firmly but not stretched: pulled fibres felt unevenly. Tuck the ends of the wisps under the layers on the soap surface so they do not unravel when wetted. Two crossing layers is enough for a first soap wrap.

Hold the wrapped bar under a gentle stream of hot water until the roving is thoroughly wet. The soap will begin to lather as water penetrates the wrapping. Squeeze gently to work the lather through all the layers. The soap supplies the washing medium; no additional soap is needed.

Rub the wet wrapped soap firmly between your palms in circular motions for 5 minutes. Work all faces: the flat top and bottom, the long sides, and the short ends. The felt will begin to tighten around the soap as the fibres interlock. Continue rubbing for 5 to 10 more minutes, adding hot water if the lather cools. The felt is ready when it no longer slides on the surface of the soap: press a fingernail gently against the felt surface; it should resist rather than push easily into the fibres.

Rinse the wrapped soap under cool water until the water runs clear and the soap has stopped lathering. Squeeze gently. Leave to dry with the wrapped soap inside on a soap dish or rack that allows air to circulate underneath. The felt will dry firmly around the soap and is ready to use in the bath or shower. When the soap inside has been used completely, the felt sleeve can be rinsed, dried, and repurposed as a small dish-scrubbing pad.

items.problem: The roving slides off the soap before felting can begin
items.cause: The initial wrapping was too loose or the wisps were pulled too thin
items.fix: Wrap more firmly and overlap the wisps by at least half their width. Tuck all loose ends under the layers before wetting.

items.problem: The felt feels loose and slides on the soap after 15 minutes of rubbing
items.cause: The fibres have not fully locked; the felt needs more agitation
items.fix: Continue rubbing. Some soaps take longer to felt around than others. If the water has cooled, add more hot water and rub for 5 more minutes.
